literal: self-extending, independent; from sva 'own' + tantra 'extension, system'
invariant_definition: Independent — possessing existence and activity not derived from any other. The principal technical use is in Madhva's foundational ontology (*Tattva-Saṅkhyāna*): there is exactly one *svatantra-tattva* — Viṣṇu — and a vast plurality of *paratantra-tattvas*, including all *jīvas*, all *cetana* and *acetana* realities, time, space, the *guṇa*s, and the *Vedas* (the last on the question of authority). The *svatantra-paratantra* distinction is one of the *pañca-bheda* in implicit form. The term is central to Dvaita; functionally analogous distinctions exist in other schools but the technical term is principally Mādhva.

per_school.definition: *Svatantra* / *paratantra* is the foundational ontological cut. *Tattva-Saṅkhyāna* opens: *svatantram paratantraṃ ca prameyam dvividhaṃ matam | svatantro bhagavān viṣṇur...* (Reality is twofold: independent and dependent. Independent is Bhagavān Viṣṇu; dependent is everything else, including the Lakṣmī...). This single cut organizes all of Madhva's metaphysics.

The functional analogue: *brahman* is the only *self-existent*; *cit* and *acit* are *aprthak-siddha* with *brahman* (V); *jīva*s are *taṭasthā-śakti* of Kṛṣṇa (A-B). The technical term *svatantra* is not foregrounded.

translator_note: 'Independent' is the standard gloss. The opposition *svatantra*-*paratantra* is structurally analogous to Spinoza's *substance*-*mode* but with the crucial difference that the *paratantra-tattvas* are real with their own existence, not modal expressions of substance.
